ex.u:
ہو گئے ہیں جمع اجزائے نگاہِ آفتاب
ذرّے اس کے گھر کی دیواروں کے روزن میں نہیں
ho gae ha͠i jamā ajzā-e nigāh-e āftāb
ex.r: zarre us ke ghar kī dīvārõ ke rauzan mẽ nahī̃
ex.e:
The parts of the gaze of the sun have become collected
There are no dust grains in the crevices of the walls of her home

روزن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روزن (rawzan, rōzan).

روزنامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روزنامه (rōznāma). By surface analysis, روز (roz) + نامَہ (nāma).

senses.g: sawm (abstaining from food, drink and sex)
a day in the month of Ramadan

روزہ.e: First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi روزہ (roza), borrowed from Classical Persian روزَه (rōza).

senses.g: a person who fasts, a fasting person
Russia (a transcontinental country in Eastern Europe and North Asia)

روس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُوس (rūs), borrowed from Arabic رُوس (rūs), from Old East Slavic Русь (Rusĭ), borrowed from Proto-Finnic *roocci, borrowed from Old East Norse *rōþs-, possibly from Proto-Germanic *rōþruz, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁reh₁-.

senses.g: to sulk, to get offended, to be upset, to be displeased

روسنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀭𑀽𑀲𑀇 (rūsaï), from Sanskrit रुष्यति (ruṣyati), from रुष् (ruṣ) or -यति (-yati). Doublet of روٹھنا.

روسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روسی (rūsī); equivalent to روس (rūs, “Russia”) + ـی (-ī)
Borrowed from Classical Persian روسی (rūsī); equivalent to روس (rūs, “Russia”) + ـی (-ī)
Borrowed from Classical Persian روسی (rūsī); equivalent to روس (rūs, “Russia”) + ـی (-ī)

senses.g: enlightened, a person who is enlightened
one who enlightens, an illuminator; a spiritual teacher, guide, or leader
God, the illuminator of all, He who created and shone His divine creative light upon all of existence

روشنی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روشنی (rawšanī, rōšanī). Equivalent to روشن (rauśan) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: the oily or fatty gravy of stew
gold, jewels, valuables, stock, etc. (as convertible into hard cash)

روکڑ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀭𑁄𑀓𑁆𑀓 (*rokka) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-), from Sanskrit रोक (roka, “buying with ready money”). Cognate with Punjabi ਰੋਕੜ (rokaṛ) / روکَڑ (rokaṛ), Sindhi روڪَڙِ (rokaṛi) / रोकड़ि, Gujarati રોકડ (rokaḍ), Marathi रोकड (rokaḍ).

senses.g: weakness (ie. a flaw in a person)

روگ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit रोग (roga), from रुज् (ruj), from Proto-Indo-European *lewǵ-. First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi روگ (rvg /⁠rog⁠/, “sorrow”).

روم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian روم (rūm, “Rome; Byzantium; Asia Minor”), from Arabic رُوم (rūm), from Middle Persian 𐭧𐭫𐭥𐭬 (ḥlʿm /⁠hrōm⁠/), from Ancient Greek Ῥώμη (Rhṓmē), from Latin Rōma.

رومال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُومَال (rūmāl, literally “face-wiper”).

رومان.e: Arabized from English romance, modeled on Arabic رُومَان (rūmān, “Romans”); both ultimately from Latin rōmānus (“Roman”). Coined in the 19th century. Doublet of رُومانْس (rūmāns).

senses.g: romance (story or novel dealing with idealized love)

رومانس.e: Borrowed from English romance. The form رُومانْس (rūmāns) is Arabised; see رُومان (rūmān).

رومی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رُومِی (rūmī), ultimately from Arabic رُومِيّ (rūmiyy). By surface analysis, رُوم (rūm) + ـِی (-ī).

رون.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa रवण (ravaṇa), from Prakrit 𑀭𑀫𑀡 (ramaṇa), from Sanskrit रमण (rámaṇa). Cognate with Gujarati રમણ (ramaṇ).

رونا.e: Inherited from Prakrit रोवइ (rovaï), रोअइ (roaï), रोयइ (royaï), from Sanskrit रोदति (rodati), रोदिति (róditi).

ex.u: اس شہر میں رونق ہی نہیں ہے۔
ex.r: is śahar mẽ koī raunaq nahī̃ hai.
ex.e: There is no splendour in this city.

senses.g: liveliness, bustlingness; flourishing state; vibe (of a gathering, party, city etc.)

ex.u: وہ ہر محفل کی رونق ہے۔
ex.r: voh har mahfil kī raunaq hai.
ex.e: They are the life of every party.

رونق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَوْنَق (rawnaq), borrowed from Arabic رَوْنَق (rawnaq). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi رونق (rvnq /⁠raunaq⁠/). Cognate with Punjabi رَوݨَق (rauṇaq).

رویہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رویه (rawiya), from رفتن (raftan, “to go”).

رہنا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi रहना (rahanā). Sense 4, the continuous aspect auxiliary, is a possible semantic loan from Persian ایستادن (istâdan), which served as the continuous aspect auxiliary in the Transoxiana dialect of Persian, a sense which is still preserved in modern Tajik истодан (istodan).

رہنما.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian راهنما (rāh-namā, literally “showing the way”).

ex.u: بادشاہانِ دہلی کے تصرف میں ملتان آگیا ، جدا ریاست نہ رہی۔
ex.r: tārīx-i hindūstān
ex.e: Multan came under the control of the Delhi rulers, it was no longer a separate state.

ریاست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریاست (riyāsat), from Arabic رِئَاسَة (riʔāsa).

ریاستہا.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Persian ریاستها (riyāsat-hā).

senses.g: America, United States of America (a country in North America; in full, United States of America)

ریاستہائے متحدہ.e: From رِیاسَت (riyāsat, “state”) + ہا (hā, “Persian plural marker”) + مُتَّحِدَہ (muttahida, “united, unified”), a calque of English United States.

ریاستی زبان.e: From ریاستی (riyāstī, “of or relating to the state (governing polity)”) + زبان (zubān).

senses.g: Riyadh (the capital city of Saudi Arabia)

ریاضت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریاضت f (riyāzat), from Arabic رِيَاضَة f (riyāḍa).

ریاضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریاضی (riyāzī), from Arabic رِيَاضِيّ (riyāḍiyy).

ریب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَیْب (rayb), from Arabic رَيْب (rayb).

senses.g: a burring guttural sound, the letter ra (as so pronounced)

ریت.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀭𑁂𑀢𑁆𑀢 (*retta), from Sanskrit रेत्र (retra), perhaps of non-Aryan origin; see there for more.

ریچھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀺𑀘𑁆𑀙 (riccha), from Sanskrit ऋक्ष (ṛkṣa), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hŕ̥ćšas, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂ŕ̥tḱos.. Doublet of خرس (xirs)

senses.g: Rekhta (A style of Urdu, Hindavi, or sometimes Braj or Punjabi used in poetry that linguistically adapts Persian grammar and vocabulary; a mix of Persian and Khariboli)

ex.u:
ريختہ کے تُم ہی اُستاد نہیں ہو غالِبؔ
کہتے ہیں اگلے زمانے میں کوئی مِیرؔ بھی تھا‬
rexta ke tumhī̃ ustād nahī̃ ho ġālib
ex.r: kahte ha͠i agle zamāne mẽ koī mīr bhī thā
ex.e:
Ghalib, you are not the only master of poetry in Rekhta
It is said that there was Mir too in the past

ریختہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریخْتَه (rēxta), form of ریخْتَن (rēxtan).

ریش.e: Derived from Classical Persian ریش (rēš, “wound, injury”).

ریشم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریشم (rēšam), a variant of ابریشم (abrēšam, abrēšum, “silk”), from Middle Persian ʾp̄lyš(w)m (abrēšom, “silk”).

ex.u: ریشمی زلفیں
ex.e: silky tresses

ex.u: ریشمی کرتا
ex.e: silk/silken kurta

ریشمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریشمی (rēšamī), a variant of ابریشمی (abrēšamī). By surface analysis, ریشم (reśam) + ـی (-ī).

ریشہ.e: PIE word *wréh₂ds Borrowed from Classical Persian ریشه (rēša, “root; stump”). Compare Baluchi ریشگ (rīšag, “root”)

ex.u: ریگستان میں بہت كم چیزیں اُگ سکتی ہیں۔
ex.r: registān mẽ bahot kam cīzẽ ug saktī h͠ai.
ex.e: Very few things can grow in the desert.

ریگستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریگِسْتَان (rēgistān). By surface analysis, ریگ (reg) + ـِستان (-istān).

ریل گاڑی.e: ریل (rel, “rail”) + گاڑی (gāṛī, “car”); calque of English railcar.

رین.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi رین (ryn) (c. 1611), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀭𑀬𑀡𑀻 (rayaṇī), from Sanskrit रजनी (rajanī́), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *Hráȷ́aniH, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *HráǰaniH, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁régʷenih₂, from *h₁régʷos (“darkness”).

رئیس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian رَئِیس (ra'īs), borrowed from Arabic رَئِيس (raʔīs).

ريح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ریح (rīh), from Arabic ريح (rīḥ).

senses.g: The fifteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ر and followed by ز.
The sixteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ڑ and followed by ژ.

زار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زار (zār), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *jāráyati.
Borrowed from English czar, from Russian царь (carʹ).

زار زار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زار زار (zār-zār).

زائر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَائِر (zā'ir), borrowed from Arabic زَائِر (zāʔir).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic
the tip (of a pen; flame of a candle etc.)

زبان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زبان (zabān, zubān), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *hijwáH, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ȷ́iȷ́ʰwáH. First attested in c. 1603 as Middle Hindi زبان (zban /⁠zabān⁠/),. Doublet of جِیبھ (jībh).

senses.g: "language of the exalted camp"; the Urdu language

زبان اردوئے معلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زُبَانِ اُرْدُویِ مُعَلّیٰ (zubān-i urdū-yi mu'allā, “language of the royal camp”), as it was believed that in Turkic camps, two languages were spoken - one being Persian, and the other what is now known as Urdu. By surface analysis, زُبَان (zubān, “language”) + اُرْدُوئے مُعَلّیٰ (urdūe mu'allā, “camp of the royals, or royal camp”).

زحمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَحْمَت (zahmat), borrowed from Arabic زَحْمَة (zaḥma), from زَحَمَ (zaḥama).

ex.u: اِتْنے وَقْت کے بَعْد میرے زَخْم بَھرے ہَیں۔
ex.r: itne vaqt ke ba'd mere zaxm bhare ha͠i.
ex.e: my wounds have healed after so much time.

زراعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِرَاعَت (zirā'at), from Arabic زِرَاعَة (zirāʕa).

زرافہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَرَافَه (zarāfa), influenced by English giraffe. Doublet of جَراف (jarāf).

زرد آلو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زردآلو (zard-ālū, literally “yellow plum”). Compare Gujarati જરદાળુ (jardāḷu), Kannada ಜರ್ದಾಳು (jardāḷu), Marathi जर्दाळू (jardāḷū), Punjabi ਜਰਦਾਲੂ (jardālū).

زعفران.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَعْفَرَان (za'farān), from Arabic زَعْفَرَان (zaʕfarān).

senses.g: saffrony — pertaining or relating to saffron
saffrony — made of or with saffron
saffrony — the color of saffron, and also translates Hindi भगवा (bhagvā)

زعفرانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَعْفَرَانِی (za'farānī), from Arabic زَعْفَرَانِيّ (zaʕfarāniyy). By surface analysis, زَعْفْرَان (za'frān) + ـِی (-ī).

زکات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَکَات (zakāt), ultimately from Arabic زَكَاة (zakāh).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

زکریا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زکریا (zakariyyā), from Arabic زَكَرِيَّا (zakariyyā).

senses.g: Almsgiving; zakat, one of the five pillars of Islam.

زکوۃ.e: Unadapted borrowing from Arabic زَكَوٰة (zakawā), specifically Classical Arabic.

زلزلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَلْزَلَه (zalzala), from Arabic زَلْزَلَة (zalzala). First attested in c. 1739 as Middle Hindi زلزلہ (zlzlh /⁠zalzala⁠/).

زلف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زُلْف (zulf, “hair, specially if dishevelled”). See the Persian entry for more.

senses.g: Zaman Park (a neighbourhood of Lahore, Punjab, Pakistan)

زمان پارک.e: Named after Post Master General of Punjab Khan Bahadur Mohammad Zaman Khan, from زَمان (zamān) + پارْک (pārk).

senses.g: vocative plural of زَمانَہ (zamāna, “era; world; reign”)
oblique plural of زَمانَہ (zamāna, “era; world; reign”)

زمانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَمَانَه (zamāna). Equivalent to زَمان (zamān) + ـہ (-h). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi زمانہ (zmanh).

senses.g: inflection of زَمانَہ (zamāna, “era; world; reign”): — direct plural
inflection of زَمانَہ (zamāna, “era; world; reign”): — oblique/vocative singular
the earth, world (as opposed to the sky, heaven)

زمین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زَمِین (zamīn). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi زمین (zmyn /⁠zamīn⁠/). Compare Punjabi زمین (zamīn), Sindhi زَمِينُ (zamīnu).

ex.u: زمین اور آسمان کا فرق
ex.r: zamīn aur āsmān kā farq
ex.e: difference of night and day

senses.g: Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see زَمِین (zamīn), آسمان (āsmān).

زن.e: PIE word *gʷḗn Borrowed from Classical Persian زن (zan, “woman; wife”). Cognate with English queen.
From Classical Persian زَن (zan, “strike; slay; beat”).

senses.g: zina, extramarital and premarital sexual intercourse

زنا.e: From Classical Persian زنا (zinā), from Arabic زِنَاء (zināʔ).

زنانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زنانه. By surface analysis, انہ + زن

زنجبیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زنجبیل (zanjabīl), from Arabic زَنْجَبِيل (zanjabīl).

زندان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِنْدَان (zindān). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi زندان (zndan /⁠zindān⁠/).

ex.u: زندگی ختم بھی ہو جائے اگر نہ کبھی ختم الفت کا سفر
ex.r: zindagī xatm bhī ho jāe agar na kabhī xatm ulfat kā safar
ex.e: Even if life comes to an end, may the journey of love never come to an end.

زندگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِنْدَگِی (zindagī). First attested in c. 1625 as Middle Hindi زندگی (zindagī).

زندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِنْدَه (zinda). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi زندہ (zndh /⁠zinda⁠/).

ex.u: ہندوستان زنده باد!
ex.r: hindūstān zinda bād!
ex.e: Long live India!

زندہ باد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زنده باد (zinda bād).

زنگبار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زنگبار (zaṅgibār). By surface analysis, زَن٘گ (zaṅg) + بار (bār).

زور.e: From Classical Persian زور (zōr, “strength, force; power, might”).
From Classical Persian زور (zōr, “strength, force; power, might”).
From Classical Persian زور (zūr, “falsehood; lie, deceit”).

زوردار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زوردار (zōrdār). Analyzed as زور (zor, “strength”) + ـدار (-dār, “having”).

senses.g: Zehri (a city in Khuzdar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
a Baloch tribe in Khuzdar district

ex.u: موٹروے پر پیش آنے والے زیادتی کیس کا حیران اور اور ڈرامائی ڈراپ سین ہو گیا۔
ex.r: Umair Badeer
ex.e: a shocking and dramatic drop in the rape case that occurred on the motorway.

ex.u: ہمارے کاموں میں جو زیادتیاں ہم سے ہو گئی ہیں اُن سے درگزر فرما
ex.r: mazāmīn abu al-kalām
ex.e: Forgive us for our transgressions which we have committed [in our deeds]

ex.u: خرپزہ ککڑی اور انگور وغیرہ بہت زیادتی کے ساتھ موجود ہیں
ex.r: sarāj-e munīr
ex.e: Melons, chickens and grapes etc. can be found in abundance

زیادتی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زیادتی (ziyādatī), from Arabic زِيَادَة (ziyāda). By surface analysis, زِیادَت (ziyādat, “increase”) + ی (ī). First attested in c. 1697 as Middle Hindi زیادتی (zyadty /⁠ziyādatī⁠/).

زیادہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زیاده (ziyāda), from Arabic زِيَادَة (ziyāda).
Borrowed from Classical Persian زیاده (ziyāda), from Arabic زِيَادَة (ziyāda).

زیارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِیَارَت (ziyārat), borrowed from Arabic زِيَارَة (ziyāra).

زیتون.e: From Arabic زَيْتُون (zaytūn) (through Classical Persian زیتون (zaytūn)).

زیربند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زیربند. By surface analysis, زیر (zer) + بند.

senses.g: a female given name, Zeenat, from Arabic

زینت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زِینَت (zīnat), borrowed from Arabic زِينَة (zīna). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi زینت (zynt /⁠zīnat⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian زِینَت (zīnat), borrowed from Arabic زِينَة (zīna). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi زینت (zynt /⁠zīnat⁠/).

زیور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian زیور (zēwar), related to زیبیدن (zēbīdan).

senses.g: The seventeenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ز and followed by س.

ژالہ باری.e: Compound of ژالَہ (źāla, “dew, hail”) + باری (bārī, “stream, flow, spate”)

senses.g: Zhob (a city in Zhob district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
disheveled, entangled, intricate, unkempt, entangled, intricate, confused

ژولیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ژولیده (žōlīda), past participle of ژولیدن (žōlīdan).

senses.g: The eighteenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ژ and followed by ش.
pertaining to the past; bygone, ancient

سابق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سابق (sābiq), from Arabic سَابِق (sābiq).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سابق (sābiq), from Arabic سَابِق (sābiq), agent noun of سَبَق (sabaq).

سات.e: Inherited from Old Hindi सात (sāta), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀢 (satta), from Sanskrit सप्त (saptá).

ساتواں.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀫 (sattama), from Sanskrit सप्तम (saptama).

ex.u: ساتھ کھاتے ہیں
ex.r: sāth khāte hẽ
ex.e: Let's eat together.

ساتھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀣 (sattha), from Sanskrit सार्थ (sā́rtha).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀣 (sattha), from Sanskrit सार्थ (sā́rtha).

ساتھی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀺𑀅 (satthia), from Sanskrit सार्थिन् (sārthin). Compare Bengali সাথী (śathi), Nepali साथि (sāthi), Marathi साथी (sāthī). Equivalent to ساتھ (sāth) + ـی (-ī).

ساجن.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀡 (sajjaṇa), from Sanskrit सज्जन (sajjana, “good man”), from सत् (sát) + जन (jána).

ساحل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَاحِل (sāhil), from Arabic سَاحِل (sāḥil).
Borrowed from Classical Persian سَاحِل (sāhil), from Arabic سَاحِل (sāḥil).

ساحلی.e: Borrowed from Arabic سَاحِلِيّ (sāḥiliyy). By surface analysis, ساحِل (sāḥil, “coast”) + ـی (-ī).

ex.u: وہ روز وَہی سادَہ کھانا کھاتے ہَیں
ex.r: vo roz vahī sāda khānā khāte ha͠i
ex.e: He eats the same simple food every day۔

ex.u: کیا تُم نے کَبھی سادَہ تِل چَٹّا دیکھا ہَے؟
ex.r: kyā tum ne kabhī sāda til caṭṭā dekhā hai?
ex.e: Have you ever seen a white cockroach?

سادہ.e: From Classical Persian ساده (sāda). Compare Bengali সাদা (śada), Gujarati સાદું (sādũ), Marathi साधे (sādhe), Punjabi ਸਾਦਾ (sādā).

ex.u:
شہر کے شوخ سادہ رو لڑکے
ظلم کرتے ہیں کیا جوانوں پر
śahar ke śox sādā-rū laṛke
ex.r: zulm karte ha͠i kyā javānõ par
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

ex.u: سارا دن
ex.r: sārā din
ex.e: all day

ex.u: سارے لوگ
ex.r: sāre log
ex.e: all of the people

سارا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀭 (sāra, “all, whole”), from Sanskrit सार (sā́ra, “best part”).

senses.g: sarangi: a kind of stringed instrument
inflection of سارا (sārā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of سارا (sārā): — oblique masculine singular/plural
sari (traditional dress of women in the Indian Subcontinent)

ساڑی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀟𑀻 (sāḍī), from Sanskrit शाटी (śāṭī), from शाट (śāṭa, “strip of cloth”). Doublet of شَال (śāl) through Persian. Compare Punjabi ਸਾੜ੍ਹੀ (sāṛhī) / ساڑھی (sāṛhī), Sindhi ساڙهيِ (sāṛhyi) / साढ़ी, Gujarati સાડી (sāḍī), Marathi साडी (sāḍī), Bengali শাড়ি (śaṛi), Assamese শাৰী (xari), Sylheti ꠢꠣꠠꠤ (haṛi), Malayalam സാരി (sāri).

senses.g: half past (mostly for hours three and up)

ساڑھے.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit [Term?], from Sanskrit सार्ध (sārdha, “plus a half”).

ساس.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ساس (sas /⁠sās⁠/) (c. 1609), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀲𑁆𑀲𑀽 (sassū), from Sanskrit श्वश्रू (śvaśrū́).

senses.g: vocative plural of ساس (sās, “mother-in-law (mother of either spouse)”)
oblique plural of ساس (sās, “mother-in-law (mother of either spouse)”)
direct plural of ساس (sās, “mother-in-law (mother of either spouse)”)

ساقی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَاقِی (sāqī), from Arabic سَاقٍ (sāqin).

senses.g: the profession of a cupbearer; cupbearer-ship, cupbearing

ex.u:
ساقی گری کی شرم کرو آج ورنہ ہم
ہر شب پیا ہی کرتے ہیں مے جس قدر ملے
sāqī-garī kī śarm karo āj varnā ham
ex.r: har śab piyā hī karte ha͠i may jis qadar mile
ex.e:
uphold the shame (the honor) of cupbearing today(,) else we
each night do drink wine of whichever quantity we (can) get

ساکشیہ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit साक्ष्य (sākṣya). Doublet of ساکھ (sākh).

senses.g: saul tree and its wood (Shorea robusta)

سالگرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَال گِرِه (sāl giri).

سالن.e: Uncertain. Some theories: * Inherited from Sanskrit *संलेपन (*saṃlepana, “smearing together”), from सम्- (sam-) + लिप् (lip, “to smear”) + -न (-na). * Inherited from Sanskrit *संलापन (*saṃlāpana), from the root ली (lī, “to attach”). * Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀮𑁄𑀡 (saloṇa), 𑀲𑀮𑀽𑀡 (salūṇa), from Sanskrit सलवण (salavaṇa, “having salt”), from स- (sa-) + लवण (lavaṇa). Compare सलोना (salonā, “tasty”).

سامان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian سَامَان (sāmān). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi ساماں (samaṉ).

سامنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀁𑀫𑀼𑀳 (saṃmuha), from Sanskrit सम्मुख (sam-mukhá, “facing”).

senses.g: a male given name, Sami, from Arabic, feminine equivalent سامِیَہ (sāmiya)
a female given name from Arabic, masculine equivalent سامی (sāmī)

سامیہ.e: Derived from Arabic سَامِيَة (sāmiya), feminine singular of سَامٍ (sāmin).

senses.g: Samba (a town and district in Jammu and Kashmir, India)

سانپ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀧𑁆𑀧 (sappa), from Sanskrit सर्प (sarpá). Compare Punjabi سَپّ (sapp) and Sindhi سَپُ (sapu).

سانس.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi سانس (sans /⁠sā̃s⁠/) (c. 1565), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀲 (sāsa), from Sanskrit श्वास (śvāsá).

senses.g: of a dark or sallow complexion

سانول.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀫𑀮 (sāmala), from Sanskrit श्यामल (śyāmalá).

سانولا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀫𑀮 (sāmala), from Sanskrit श्यामल (śyāmalá).

senses.g: Shraavana (the fifth month in the Hindu Calendar)

ساون.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi ساون (savn /⁠sāvan⁠/) (c. 1780), from Prakrit 𑀲𑀸𑀯𑀡 (sāvaṇa), from Sanskrit श्रावण (śrā́vaṇa). Cognate with Bengali শাওন (śaōn), Gujarati સાવણ (sāvaṇ), Punjabi ਸਉਣ (sauṇa) / ساون (sāvn), Sindhi سانوڻ (sānoṇ) / सांवणु (sā̃vaṇu). Compare Pashto ساوڼ (sāwaṇ).

senses.g: association, connection, combination, union, society, fellowship
Sahiwal (a city in Sahiwal district, Punjab, Pakistan)
